greeting: Morning, Haniel. CT Pros gets a real home today — let's make it count.
hype: You're planting the flag for CT Pros today: fresh server, real QB account, clean catalog. Four go-live blocks close it out. GS gets three sharp analytical pushes. Seven blocks, zero blockers — move.
notice: Have CT Pros QB credentials and the domain registrar login from Rafa ready before 9 AM — they unlock your first three blocks.

schedule.title: Fill real CT Pros company contact info
Design + installation time benchmarks per project type
Connect CT Pros’s own QuickBooks (fresh OAuth)
Deploy CT Pros to its own host/domain
Re-curate material catalog for a GC
Owner notification panel for revision_runaway events

tasks.title: Design + installation time benchmarks per project type
tasks.notes: KB 9.1: Track next 10 projects for design time. Interview install crew leads for installation times. Required for capacity planning + accurate scheduling.

prep.what: Build the data-capture mechanism for design and install time benchmarks — the time-standard backbone every scheduler and capacity planner reads from.
prep.understand: `TimeStandard` already exists (KB 9.1 / Module 7). Two tracks: (a) a per-project design-time logging pipeline for the next 10 projects, and (b) initial install-time benchmarks derived from crew lead interviews. Accurate standards are the difference between a schedule that holds and one that slips every week.
approach: Read the existing `TimeStandard` model/migration and how `ProductionPriorityService` consumes it for scheduling decisions.
Design the capture schema: a `DesignTimeLog` table (project_id, phase, started_at, ended_at, designer_id) to track actuals per project going forward.
Draft a structured interview script for install crew leads: per project type, average install days per sqft or unit.
Seed provisional benchmark values from the last 10 completed projects and flag them `provisional=true` so the refinement loop knows to update them.
resources: Historical completed-project data from the GS dashboard

tasks.title: QB integration mapping documentation (estimate fields, pricing, classes)
tasks.notes: KB 12.1. Pull sample estimate from QB and document every field used. Improves estimate auto-parsing + line-item structuring.

prep.what: Pull a real GS Woodworking estimate from QuickBooks and document how every QB field maps to the CRM — catching schema mismatches before they silently corrupt production syncs.
prep.understand: The QB integration is battle-tested against mocks (QBEstimateSyncTest has 9 cases). But GS’s real QB estimates may use custom classes, item naming, or field formats the mocks don’t capture. One undocumented mismatch = silent wrong data on every sync.
approach: Log into GS’s QB Online (or sandbox) and pull the raw API response for a recent sent estimate via the QB API Explorer or a logged sync.
List every field in the response: Ref#, TxnDate, line items (Description, DetailType, Amount, ItemRef, ClassRef), CustomerRef, TxnStatus, ExchangeRate.
Map each QB field to its CRM counterpart in `QBEstimateSync::push()` — flag any gaps, name mismatches, or type differences.
Save the result as `docs/qb-estimate-field-map.md` with a field-by-field mapping table.
resources: QB credentials for GS’s sandbox or production account

tasks.title: Owner notification panel for revision_runaway events (Phase D follow-on)
tasks.notes: Phase D fires Spatie ActivityLog rows with event=revision_runaway when an item’s revision_round hits 5. UI gap: subscribe in Owner dashboard sidebar.

prep.what: Wire the owner dashboard sidebar to display `revision_runaway` ActivityLog alerts — items that hit 5 revision rounds already trigger these events but surface nowhere in the UI.
prep.understand: Phase D already calls `activity()->log('revision_runaway')` when `revision_round` hits 5. The ActivityLog rows exist in the DB. The gap is purely UI — no new events or DB schema needed.
approach: Locate the owner dashboard controller/component and find existing notification or alert feed patterns to reuse.
Add a query: `Activity::where('event', 'revision_runaway')->whereDate('created_at', today())->latest()->get()` (adjust for your ActivityLog query API).
Render each result as an alert tile in the sidebar: item name, revision count, timestamp.
Test by inserting a fake `revision_runaway` log entry via `php artisan tinker` and confirming the sidebar shows it.
resources: Phase D event-firing code (grep codebase for `revision_runaway`)
Owner dashboard Blade or Vue component

tasks.title: Fill real CT Pros company contact info
tasks.notes: config/company.php + .env still hold placeholders inherited from the clone. Set real CT Pros name/address/phone/email BEFORE any client-facing PDF or email goes out.

prep.what: Update `config/company.php` and `.env` to replace every HighTech clone placeholder with real CT Pros contact info.
prep.understand: Every client-facing PDF and outbound email renders these fields at generation time. Shipping a proposal or invoice with ‘HighTech’ or placeholder data on it is a trust failure before CT Pros even launches. Zero-placeholder rule before go-live.
approach: Open `config/company.php` — replace name, address, phone, email, tagline with real CT Pros values (ask Rafa if not on hand).
Update `.env`: APP_NAME, MAIL_FROM_ADDRESS, MAIL_FROM_NAME to reflect CT Pros branding.
Grep the codebase for ‘HighTech’, ‘placeholder’, ‘example.com’ in config and .env files to catch any stragglers.
Re-seed company settings if the seeder reads from config, then spot-check a rendered PDF for the updated branding.
resources: CT Pros real contact details (name, address, phone, email) — ask Rafa
CompanySettingsSeeder (if it reads from config at seed time)

tasks.title: Connect CT Pros’s own QuickBooks (fresh OAuth)
tasks.notes: Inherited tokens were HighTech’s and were never carried over. Run a fresh OAuth connect against CT Pros’s own QBO company so all syncs hit the right books.

prep.what: Run the QB OAuth connect flow for CT Pros’s own QBO account, replacing any stale HighTech realm tokens in the app.
prep.understand: `QuickBooksToken` stores the QBO realm ID that identifies which company’s books are synced. With HighTech’s realm still in there, every CT Pros sync silently pushes to the wrong books — a financial integrity blocker for go-live. The auth flow is fully built; this is an ops/credentials task.
approach: Confirm `QUICKBOOKS_CLIENT_ID` and `QUICKBOOKS_CLIENT_SECRET` in CT Pros’s `.env` belong to CT Pros’s Intuit developer app — not HighTech’s (get from Rafa).
Navigate to `/settings/quickbooks`, disconnect any existing token, then click Connect to start the OAuth flow.
Complete the OAuth redirect → callback; verify the stored `QuickBooksToken` has CT Pros’s QBO realm ID.
Run `php artisan quickbooks:sync` manually and confirm the logs reference CT Pros’s company, not HighTech’s realm.
resources: CT Pros Intuit developer credentials — get from Rafa
QuickBooksToken model (encrypted casts + realm ID field)

tasks.title: Deploy CT Pros to its own host/domain
tasks.notes: Deploy infra proven (deploy/*.sh, docker-compose.yml, docs/deployment.md) but still pointed at sys.hightechtileinc.com / droplet 104.248.228.169. Re-target to a fresh DO droplet or Hostinger with a fresh DB and CT Pros’s real domain.

prep.what: Provision a fresh server for CT Pros, point the real domain at it, and run the first full deployment from scratch.
prep.understand: The deploy infra (`deploy/*.sh`, `docker-compose.yml`, `docs/deployment.md`) is proven from HighTech — use it as the exact playbook. But CT Pros needs complete isolation: do NOT reconfigure or reuse HighTech’s droplet (104.248.228.169) or domain. Fresh server, fresh DB, fresh domain.
approach: Provision a new DO droplet (or Hostinger VPS) matching HighTech’s proven specs — read `docs/deployment.md` first for exact distro/size/packages.
Update CT Pros’s DNS: point the real domain’s A record at the new server IP. (Allow 5–15 min for propagation.)
SSH in, clone the CT Pros repo, copy `.env.production` from the template — set APP_URL to CT Pros domain, fresh DB credentials, CT Pros’s mail/QB keys (from t24 and t25).
Run `deploy/deploy.sh` (or `docker-compose up`), run migrations, seed, and verify the app boots at the domain with valid HTTPS.
resources: docs/deployment.md (read this first — exact server playbook)
CT Pros domain registrar dashboard — login from Rafa
.env.production template (copy from HighTech, update every field)

tasks.title: Re-curate material catalog for a GC
tasks.notes: The 148-SKU HighTech TILE catalog still seeds — wrong domain. Replace with a general-contractor-appropriate catalog before takeoff pricing or estimates are trusted.

prep.what: Replace the 148-SKU HighTech tile catalog in the CT Pros seeder with a GC catalog covering painting and flooring materials with realistic pricing tiers.
prep.understand: CT Pros is a GC specializing in painting and flooring — the tile catalog is wrong-domain. Every estimate or takeoff that pulls from it generates wrong line items and prices. The `CuratedMaterialSeeder` structure exists; this is DATA replacement, not an architecture change.
approach: Open `database/seeders/CuratedMaterialSeeder.php` — understand the schema: category, name, unit, coverage/waste factor, and low/mid/high pricing tiers.
Draft the GC catalog: painting (Interior Latex, Exterior Latex, Primer, Ceiling Paint — per gallon with coverage factors) + flooring (LVP, Carpet, Hardwood, Tile — per sqft with waste factors).
Replace the seeder arrays with the new catalog; add supplier refs where known (Sherwin-Williams for paint).
Run `php artisan db:seed --class=CuratedMaterialSeeder` locally, open an estimate, and confirm the line-item picker shows GC-relevant materials priced correctly.
resources: Rafa’s standard material suppliers and approximate price list — ask if not on hand
Existing category structure in the DB (check /catalog in the app)
